entries.body: The US market access problem for foreign robot hardware is unsolved and wide open. Robotics companies don't want to build their own cyber compliance and FedRAMP certification stack. Whoever builds the wrapper layer — handles the compliance, certifies the hardware — controls the bottleneck into the US market. Classic infrastructure play hiding in plain sight.

Structuring matters as much as building for China supply chain access. Foreign companies can't easily own their manufacturing relationships in China. The structural play is a Chinese entity with a US subsidiary as the public face — several operators are doing this quietly.
RaaS is getting institutional traction. A fund is actively financing Fanuc deployments on a robotics-as-a-service model. The financing layer is maturing — this is no longer just hardware sales.

Hotel operators are becoming early robotics adopters. One rollup focused on hotel operational efficiency is already in active deployment conversations with a Miami property. Physical AI is finding PMF in hospitality before most people expect it.

Off-the-shelf batteries are becoming the default power stack for humanoid robots. Companies are increasingly skipping custom power systems and pulling from commodity industrial suppliers — DeWalt packs (Stanley Black & Decker, SWK) are showing up directly in builds. Creates real tailwinds for the industrial battery stack: BorgWarner (BWA) is the one we keep flagging, alongside SWK as the upstream beneficiary.
